title: Let Go My Lego – Young Children (Ages 6–8)

hunks.text: LEGO bricks are colorful building blocks. Kids love to BUILD with LEGO. You can make houses, cars, and towers. LEGO bricks snap together and come apart easily. When you want to stop building, you LET GO of your LEGO.

questions.question: What are LEGO bricks?
What can you make with LEGO?
Name something you can build with LEGO.
Imagine you are holding a red LEGO brick. What do you feel in your hand?

hunks.text: Sometimes building with LEGO is hard work. Your hands might get tired. You might feel STUCK when LEGO pieces do not come apart. When this happens, it is okay to take a BREAK. Taking a break helps your hands feel better.

questions.question: What might happen to your hands when building?
What should you do when your hands are tired?
When you take a break, what would you like to do?
Close your eyes. Feel your hands holding LEGO. What do they feel?

hunks.text: Sharing LEGO is NICE. When your friend wants to build too, you can SHARE your bricks. You can work together and make something BIG and COOL. Sharing makes playtime more FUN for everyone.

questions.question: What is it called when you give your friend some LEGO?
What happens when friends build together?
What word describes building together with a friend?
Who would you like to build LEGO with?

hunks.text: Sometimes you build something you LOVE. You want to keep it forever. But LEGO creations do not have to last forever. You can TAKE APART what you made and BUILD something new. Letting go of your old creation helps you make new ideas.

questions.question: What can you do with an old LEGO creation?
After you take apart your LEGO, what can you make?
Imagine you took apart your favorite LEGO house. What do you see?
What new thing would you like to build?

hunks.text: Letting go can feel SAD at first. You worked hard on your LEGO house or tower. It is okay to feel SAD when you take it apart. But soon you will have FUN making something else. Feelings change quickly when you play.

questions.question: How might you feel when you take apart your LEGO?
Have you ever felt sad about something? Tell me about it.
Close your eyes. Remember a time you took something apart. What do you feel?
What happens to your sad feeling soon?

hunks.text: Learning to let go of LEGO teaches you something important. It teaches you that change is okay. You learn that you can CREATE new things. Letting go is not about losing. It is about GROWING and making room for new ideas.

questions.question: What does letting go teach you about?
What can you make when you let go and start fresh?
Name something you learn from letting go of LEGO.
questions.answer: CHANGE IS OKAY / YOU CAN CREATE / MAKE ROOM

questions.question: What is something new you have learned to make?

hunks.text: You can save special LEGO creations before you take them apart. You can take a PHOTO of your building. You can ask a grown-up to help you draw a PICTURE of it. This way, you remember your creation without keeping the bricks.

questions.question: What can you take of your LEGO to remember it?
Who can help you draw your LEGO building?
Name a way to remember your LEGO creation.
Imagine you are looking at a photo of your LEGO house. What do you see?

hunks.text: Letting go of LEGO is a SKILL you can practice. Every time you take apart your building, you get better at it. You learn that new ideas are waiting. You feel PROUD when you make something different. Letting go helps you become brave and STRONG.

questions.question: What do you get better at when you practice letting go?
What feeling comes when you make something new?
What makes you feel proud?
Close your eyes. Imagine you finished something new with LEGO. How does your body feel?

citations: LEGO.com: "The History of LEGO Bricks and Creative Play" – Official LEGO educational resources on building and creativity (2024)
American Psychological Association: "Growth Mindset in Children" – Research on learning through play and persistence (2023)
Child Mind Institute: "Teaching Children to Let Go and Adapt to Change" – Developmental psychology articles on emotional resilience (2023)
